column2: — Lilya, sit down, — said Miyechka.
— I'll bring one for myself.
— Dear, sit here!
— cried Mrs. Darochka, trying to spring up from her seat.
— No need!
— Miyechka called and quickly went off to the house again.
On the way she cast a glance at Ustym; he sat on his knees with the drill.
From the house she took another stool, a cloth, and as many cups as she could.
When Miyechka came back, Mrs. Darochka and the grandmother were just then discussing where the craftsman was from and whether he was a non-believer.
— Wherever did you find one like that, who'd just up and come right on Ivan Kupala?
— asked Mrs. Darochka.
— Solomiya summoned him, — the grandmother retorted.
— So maybe he's a shtunda?
— Mrs. Darochka pressed.
— Not a shtunda, — Marta cut in.
— He said he'd be drinking.
— Aha, so he's one of those in rehab?
One of the drug addicts?
— Mrs. Darochka, between alcoholics and shtundas there are intermediate values as well, — Marta snapped.
Lilichka went into the house and brought a teapot of tea, sliced fresh bread, cheese with herbs, tomatoes and little toasts with pâté.
— Ustym, — Miyechka called, coming up to the roof.
He looked down at her from above.
Solomiya stood there so homey and cozy in her blue dress with bare shoulders and her hair pinned up.
— Miyechka...
— he said.
— Take a break.
— But I've still got half an hour here.
— Come down to us, you'll finish later.
Ustym came down to Miyechka, raised his hand to her shoulder and lowered it.
— Let's go, — he said.
— So whose are you?
— Mrs. Darochka asked Ustym before he'd even reached the table.
— I'm not from around here, — Ustym retorted with a broad smile.
It dazzled everyone.
— And do you always drink your tea like this, Solomiya, in the weeds under the bushes?
— Ustym asked, glancing around.
— Well, you have to be kept an eye on, — the grandmother spoke up.
— Or else you'll do it too well.
— And we need a rustic setting, — said Lilichka.
— So you're a non-believer?
— Mrs. Darochka simply couldn't let it rest.
Ustym looked at her, smiled again his broad, white-toothed smile, and said gently:
— I, madam, work by blessing.
— A-ha-ha-ha, — Mrs. Darochka nodded.
— It's like how, even on holy days, you can hew coffins, if you must.
— Exactly, — Ustym retorted.
— That's just what I asked: 'Give me, Father, I said, the kind of blessing you give for coffins.'
— And did he?
— asked Marta, holding back laughter.
— In the name of the Father and the Son, — said Ustym and smiled again.
— A-ha-ha-ha, — Mrs. Darochka nodded again, adjusting her headscarf, and, casting a glance at Marta, started talking again.
— Your Martochka, I see, is in the family way.
— God granted it, God granted it, — the grandmother nodded.
Miyechka watched Ustym as he ate — he always ate in a hurry, without stopping, throwing glances around.
Lilichka tried to reach her sister's foot under the table, to give her a signal to stop staring.
But it was already too late.
Maria was already looking at Miyechka, and at Maria — Teodora.
— And who are you expecting, dear?
— Mrs. Darochka kept on questioning.
— I'm expecting a daughter, — retorted Marta.
— But there'll be two sons.
— A daughter is a blessing, dear, — retorted Mrs. Darochka.
Ustym meanwhile got up from the table.
— Maybe with the midday sun like this, you shouldn't?
— said Miyechka.
— Yes, sweetheart, better to wait for the rain, — answered Ustym.

He worked on the roof again, while six women sat in the bushes, finishing their tea and watching how the work was coming along, until a seventh little girl appeared on the threshold in nothing but a little undershirt.
Barefoot, bewildered and sleepy, Lusya stood on the steps.
— Oh, and whose child is this?
— Mrs. Darochka threw up her hands.
— Yours, Miyechka?
— asked Mrs. Darochka.
— Not yet, — she answered quickly.
— Then yours, Lilya?
Lilichka grew flustered, glanced at Marta, and the grandmother retorted at once:
— Ours!
While everyone was sorting out whose child it was, Marta was already kneeling beside Lusya, saying something to her.
Lusya smiled, showing a row of small white teeth.
And Marta took the little girl by the hand and led her to the table.
Lusya stepped carefully across the grass on her bare little feet.
She already knew what a nettle was, and before each step she watched out for whether any stinging leaves were hiding somewhere in the grass.
Ustym had only just finished mending the roof when clouds began to gather.
A fine rain set in.
Darkness was descending.
— We should have waited, right, Miyechka?
— asked Ustym.
— Let me wash your T-shirt for you, — said Miyechka.
— Since when do you have such desires?
Miyechka smiled.
Everyone had long since fled into the house, carried in the cloths, mugs and biscuits, the barefoot child, and seen off the inquisitive Mrs. Darochka.
Fine drops beat against the roof.
The last thing Miyechka wanted was for Ustym to leave.
The last thing Ustym wanted was to leave.
— Solomiya!
— came the grandmother's voice from the threshold.
Solomiya came up to Ustym, embraced him, felt under her hands those ever-tense muscles.
She didn't know whether he was always like that, or only when she was near.
— Solomiya!
— came the voice again.
She pressed her forehead to his collarbones.
He was finally stroking her shoulders.
Grabbing Ustym's hand, she quickly kissed it and ran to the threshold.
The wet jasmine leaves slapped against her bare shoulders.
— Granny, what?
— she said.
— I didn't hear you.
— Has he left already?
Miyechka darted into the house, there were wet patches on her dress, the rain was getting stronger and stronger.
By the window stood Lilichka, having set Lusya on the table.
The little girl looked at the rain and chewed on her little undershirt.
— Mia, so has he left already?
— the grandmother repeated.
— No, not yet, he's getting ready to.
The grandmother came up to Miyechka and whispered:
— So have you settled up with him?
Lilichka snorted.
— Granny, that's my business, don't you worry about it.
— Then let me give some money.
— Granny, really, I'll handle it myself, — retorted Miyechka.
— I'm just thinking that you should give him more, — the grandmother said seriously.
Lilichka snorted again, she and Miyechka caught each other's eyes, and Miyechka couldn't hold back her laughter.
— Girls, what's so funny?
— the grandmother went on.
— He also did the bit over the veranda for us, and there was no talk of that.
— It's just that Lusya over there is chewing her undershirt so funnily, — Lilichka changed the subject.
Lusya stood on the table and sucked her undershirt with concentration.
Marta had given her a ballerina's hairdo.
She looked older, but very tiny.
— Funny indeed, — said the grandmother.
— Find the child a pacifier, or she'll eat up all her clothes.
Ustym appeared outside the window, holding a jacket over his head.
The grandmother, pushing Miyechka aside, opened the door and spoke quickly:
— Do come in, come in!
Ustym glanced at Miyechka, threw the jacket off his head and came inside.
— I wanted to take the keys, — he said.
— I hung them right here, — Miyechka rushed, grabbing the keys.
— I'll see you off, — she said and darted out into the yard.
— Throw something on!
— the grandmother shouted after her.
Miyechka came into the house about ten minutes later.
There was no one left in the corridor.
Outside the rain roared.
The silver car still flickered before her eyes, her hands were still warm, because she had just been holding them under his jacket.
In the house there was an eerie silence.
She went into the kitchen, it was gray and dark there, only the little flame in the gas boiler flickered.
Only after leaving the kitchen did she hear muffled voices somewhere in the far room.
On the wall hung a mirror of unknown origin, framed in a heavy wooden frame.
Once she and Lilichka had cleaned that frame with rubbing alcohol and matchsticks.
Miyechka loved monotonous work, when, millimeter by millimeter, shine and cleanliness spread out.
In the mirror she saw a woman with wet hair tucked behind her ears.
Not all of the hair fit behind the ears, and some of it stuck out.
Miyechka looked at her own palms and pressed them to her cheeks, pulling the skin away from her eyes.
In her eyes there were tiny blood vessels.
— Are you here already?
— Lilichka whispered.
She stood in the doorway to the room.
— Mm-hm, — said Miyechka, without taking her hands from her face.
— Are you okay?
— Yeah, — she answered.
— Come on.
— Let's go.
Miyechka followed her sister into the farthest little room with the way out to the garden.
There, on the little bed, slept Lusya.
Beside her sat Marta and the grandmother.
Maria was leaning on the little table by the window.
— What are you all doing here?
— asked Miyechka.
— Seeing whether your craftsman's hands grow from the right place, — retorted the grandmother.
— And how is it?
— So far it's not leaking, — said Maria.
— Did you see him off?
— He's gone.
The roof did not leak.
The rain pelted down all night without a break.
Marta, having gathered up her suddenly grown-out dark, heavy hair, lay beside Lusya.
The wall was dry.
The rain fell.
The Match
Miyechka couldn't sleep from five in the morning.
Something kept appearing to her, hounding her, smothering her like a dark toad.
She kept remembering that T-shirt and the line between it and the belt of his jeans, she thought about how Danylo had said to her: 'Do you need freedom, little bird?
Then take as much of it as you want.'
But is Ustym really freedom?
This is an even greater unfreedom than Danylo with his soapy smell and neutral facial features.
If anyone had asked her to describe him, she would have said that he has eyes, a nose, lips, and that all this together adds up to a plain human face.
It was still dark when she got up from bed and went to the kitchen.
She didn't turn on the electric kettle, so it wouldn't make noise.

Instead she poured herself water into a little saucepan and stood with it by the stove, watching how the fire wrapped its tongues around the metal sides of the ladle, and waited for the water to hiss, rising up the heated walls.
Miyechka wore the T-shirt of some long-ago visitor to this house — large, roomy, with the crest of some American university.
— What are you doing?
